summary: Gruber reflects on the Apple Watch's troubled debut and how WatchOS 3 and Series 2 hardware represent Apple honestly confronting its mistakes. He argues Apple misjudged the Watch's core value proposition, wrongly emphasizing social communication features (heartbeats, doodles) while underinvesting in what actually mattered: health and fitness tracking. The original WatchOS was too slow, the UI was convoluted, and the hardware had real shortcomings in brightness and GPS. WatchOS 3 fixes most software problems — dramatically improving performance, replacing the failed communication button with a useful Dock, and improving workout controls — while Series 2 addresses hardware gaps with a brighter screen, built-in GPS, and swim capability. Gruber sees Apple's willingness to admit conceptual mistakes as a healthy sign for the company's culture.
key_points: Apple's original three tentpole features for Apple Watch were wrong: the 'revolutionary new way to connect with others' was a complete bust that wasted the side button and convoluted the entire UI
WatchOS 3 dramatically improves performance by being less stingy with RAM, proving the original hardware was more capable than the software allowed it to appear
The correct tentpole functions are: stylish timepiece, notification/response extension of iPhone, and comprehensive health and fitness companion
Apple's willingness to swallow pride and admit conceptual mistakes with WatchOS 3 is a sign of healthy internal culture, unlike the iPhone 7 which required no such humility
Series 2 hardware fixes remaining gaps: doubled screen brightness, built-in GPS, swim capability, and better battery life
money_quotes: I don't feel bad about not quite getting Apple Watch at first, though. Apple didn't get it either.
The way to be right all the time is not to be right all the time, because that never happens.
The way to be right all the time is to be smart enough to be right most of the time, and humble enough to recognize your mistakes and address them.
Nobody used that stuff. The best way to text someone from Apple Watch wasn't to use that button, it was to use Siri.
The original hardware was more capable than the software team expected, which led them to write software that resulted in the hardware feeling less capable.
You need to put ego aside to admit that the whole 'revolutionary new way to connect with others' wasn't revolutionary at all, but instead a mistake at the very conceptual root of the UI.
If you're pushing the boundaries of any endeavor, mistakes are inevitable. If you convince yourself that you're right all the time, you'll slip into denial regarding your mistakes.
key_insight: Apple's willingness to admit the Apple Watch's conceptual mistakes and fundamentally rethink the product — rather than doubling down — demonstrates the kind of institutional humility that separates companies that iterate toward greatness from those that compound their errors.
